coredata.dc:title: Ratiometric fluorescence quantitation of amoxicillin based on CDs@Eu-MOFs incorporated 3D hydrogel using smartphone-assisted portable dual mode visual sensing
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Azeez, Kurdistan Fakhraldin
Salimi, Abdollah
Mohtasham, Hamed
coredata.dc:description: Amoxicillin (AMX) is commonly used antibiotic with a broad activity against Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria. Therefore, it is crucial access to an instant and real-time method for accurate and correct AMX determination. Here, we have developed a three-dimensional hydrogel modified with Europium -Metal organic frameworks (Eu-MOFs) and carbon dots (CDs) as fluorophores, which allows for ratiometric fluorescence detection of AMX. The Eu-MOFs and CDs as an AMX probe trapped in Carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) based hydrogel. In the presence of AMX, the fluorescence intensity of CDs@Eu-MOFs, enhanced at 448 nm and decreased at 568 nm under 360 nm excitation, due to energy transfer process. Under optimal conditions, AMX determined in the linear range of 10 μM to 106.7 μM, with a detection limit of 1.17 μM in liquid state. While, in the hydrogel state, the linear range for AMX detection is from 0.3 μM to 3.07 μM, with a detection limit of 0.08 μM. So, through applying hydrogel state, assay sensitivity is increased 32-times compared with conventional using liquid state. Furthermore, the change of the fluorescence color under UV irradiation also applied for colorimetric sensing of AMX at concentration up to 106 μM with detection limit 0.63 μM using smartphone RGB color sensing software. In addition, presented assay indicates strong selectivity for AMX over other biomolecules, salts and also other antibiotics. Also, it successfully detects different quantities of AMX in water and milk samples with excellent sensitivity, precision, and reliability in both liquid and hydrogel states.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Amoxicillin
Europium-metal organic frameworks
Carbon dots
Ratiometric fluorescence assay
Hydrogel
Visual sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of yellow emitting carbon dots (CDs)
Detection of AMX in water and milk samples
Optical properties, photostability and dispersity of CDs@Eu-MOFs
The ratiometric fluorescence sensing of AMX in both liquid and hydrogel states
The visualization and prompt identification of AMX
Detection of amoxicillin in samples of milk and water

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of CDs. (B) SEM image of Eu-MOFs. (C) Different excitation of CDs (D) 2D excitation of CDs. (E) Different excitation of Eu-MOFs. (F) 2D excitation of Eu-MOFs.

(A) Different excitation of CDs@Eu-MOFs in liquid state (B) Different excitation of CDs@Eu-MOFs in hydrogel state (C) 2D excitation of CDs@Eu-MOFs. (D, E) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs, Eu-MOFs and CDs@Eu-MOFs in liquid and hydrogel state. (F) Digital photographs of CMC, CDs@Eu-MOFs-CMC and AMX-CDs@Eu-MOFs-CMC under visible (left side) and UV light (right side).

Fluorescence spectrum of CDs@Eu-MOFs with increasing AMX (A) Concentrations (0–106.7μM) upon excitation at 360 nm in liquid state. (B) Concentration (0–3.07 μM) in hydrogel-state.(C) The calibration depicting the Relationship between I448/I568 and AMX concentration is accompanied by (an inset image that displays the corresponding fluorescence picture captured under a 365 nm UV lamp) in liquid state. (D) Calibration curve of hydrogel-state CDs@Eu-MOFs with the increasing of AMX concentration. (E, F) Time effect the stability of AMX-CDs@Eu-MOFs for hydrogel and liquid states.

(A) The illustration showcases the portable sensing platform based on smartphones. (B) The correlation between the G/mean(R + G + B) values of the liquid state and the concentration of AMX is demonstrated. The inset exhibits fluorescence images captured under a 365 nm UV light, showcasing the progression of AMX concentrations up to 105 μM.(C,D) Upon optimizing the conditions, the fluorescence response of CDs@Eu-MOFs was evaluated to assess the selectivity of the method in both liquid and hydrogel states.
Comparison of different methods for AMX sensing.

row.entry: CDs@Eu-MOFs in liquid and hydrogel state
10–106.7 uM in liquid state and 0.3–3.07 μM

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The determination and recovery test conducted on AMX spiked in a real sample.

head.ce:title: Ratiometric fluorescence quantitation of amoxicillin based on CDs@Eu-MOFs incorporated 3D hydrogel using smartphone-assisted portable dual mode visual sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of Kurdistan, 66177-15175 Sanandaj, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aDepartment of Chemistry, University of Kurdistan, 66177-15175 Sanandaj, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science and Health, Koya University, Koya KOY45, Kurdistan Region, Iraq
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bDepartment of Chemistry, Faculty of Science and Health, Koya University, Koya KOY45, Kurdistan Region, Iraq

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center for Nanotechnology, University of Kurdistan, 66177-15175 Sanandaj, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cResearch Center for Nanotechnology, University of Kurdistan, 66177-15175 Sanandaj, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Amoxicillin (AMX) is commonly used antibiotic with a broad activity against Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria. Therefore, it is crucial access to an instant and real-time method for accurate and correct AMX determination. Here, we have developed a three-dimensional hydrogel modified with Europium -Metal organic frameworks (Eu-MOFs) and carbon dots (CDs) as fluorophores, which allows for ratiometric fluorescence detection of AMX. The Eu-MOFs and CDs as an AMX probe trapped in Carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) based hydrogel. In the presence of AMX, the fluorescence intensity of CDs@Eu-MOFs, enhanced at 448 nm and decreased at 568 nm under 360 nm excitation, due to energy transfer process. Under optimal conditions, AMX determined in the linear range of 10 μM to 106.7 μM, with a detection limit of 1.17 μM in liquid state. While, in the hydrogel state, the linear range for AMX detection is from 0.3 μM to 3.07 μM, with a detection limit of 0.08 μM. So, through applying hydrogel state, assay sensitivity is increased 32-times compared with conventional using liquid state. Furthermore, the change of the fluorescence color under UV irradiation also applied for colorimetric sensing of AMX at concentration up to 106 μM with detection limit 0.63 μM using smartphone RGB color sensing software. In addition, presented assay indicates strong selectivity for AMX over other biomolecules, salts and also other antibiotics. Also, it successfully detects different quantities of AMX in water and milk samples with excellent sensitivity, precision, and reliability in both liquid and hydrogel states.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: For combating bacterial infections in the fields of agriculture, animal husbandry and medicine, Antibiotics, which are widely used [1]. β-lactam antibiotics, Including amoxicillin (AMX), is a main commercial antibiotic in the world, because of their strong effect on extensive range of bacterial and various microorganisms [2,3]. Different common methods such as electrochemical [4], LC-MS [5], HPLC [6], Fluorescence [7–9] have been utilized for the detection of amoxicillin in food products, pharmaceutical formulations and urine samples. Some of the employed techniques in the detection process, may have disadvantages such as complex synthesis procedures and time-consuming sample pretreatment. Nevertheless, due to simple sample preparation, high degree of sensitivity, selectivity and rapid reaction time of fluorescent methods, these probes are widely used in medical diagnosis and biological analysis [10,11]. Nevertheless, it is worth noting that the most of reported fluorescent sensors for their analytical response depends on a single wavelength. This methods performance of a singular wavelength is sensitive to change in environmental factors, instrument efficiency and probe concentration. To overcome these limitations, the ratiometric fluorescence methods are being used. Due to reduction of environmental fluctuations and background interference in the ratiometric fluorescence methods, the accuracy and reliability of detection results are significantly improved [12–14]. However, these ways are commonly designed for single-signal detection in the environments of the laboratory. Dual-mode detection is more dependable compared to single-mode detection method because it provides additional data, reduce external disturbances [15]. The dual-mode techniques are consisted of Fluorescence Surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) [16], fluorescence-colorimetric [17], fluorescence-electrochemistry [18] and so forth. In fluorescence-colorimetric (dual-mode) our unaided eyes are unable to distinguish minute color changes [19] so this method combined with smartphone software technology as a novel approach for quick and visual target identification [20].

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) represent a novel class of nanoporous materials [21]. The distinctive properties of MOFs arise from their high surface area, varied structural configurations, and remarkable adaptability [22]. Lanthanide-based metal-organic frameworks (Ln-MOFs) are distinguished category of luminescent materials are highly promising materials in fluorescence sensors development because of unique luminescence properties including strong Stokes shift, small characteristic emission peak and long fluorescence life-time [23].

Carbon Dots (CDs) is zero-dimensional (0D) carbon nanomaterials with sizes below 10 nm [24]. Aside CDs having remarkable optical characteristics same with quantum dots also provide advantages such as superior dispersion, excellent physical and chemical properties biocompatibility, a broad excitation spectrum, hydrophilicity and low toxicity [25–27]. As a result, CDs present promising futures in the various domains such as photocatalysis [28]. Sensing [29], medication delivery, antimicrobial, cancer treatment, and bioimaging [30,31]. The most of CD-based fluorescence sensing systems detect analytes in aqueous solutions, however in new gel-based or solid-state sensor systems have been provided the possibility of CDs fixing inside hydrogel matrices for sensing applications [32–34]. The hydrogels are three-dimensional extended networks made of tiny organic, polymeric, and inorganic building blocks that are physically entangled and interact noncovalently [35]. The usage of hydrogels as support matrix in biosensing applications, provides further advantages due to the presence of pores. These pores not only facilitate the diffusion of the analyte from the surrounding solution to the internal sites which allowing for fast and optimal access to the analyte but also increase the effective surface area. Another potential strategy to address the previous issue is the incorporation of CDs into a solid matrix to create a composite material [36]. As a result, modification and integration of fluorescent nanoparticles into a hydrogel framework, a three-dimensional structure is formed that can interact with water-soluble analytes and facilitates the process of quantitative sensing. These hydrogels have the potential to improve either liquid or solid substrates, or even combine the benefits of both [37,38].

In this study, we developed a dual-mode fluorescence and colorimetric method for liquid and hydrogel states. The result ratiometric fluorescence-based smart hydrogel probe was used for detecting and quantifying AMX. A ratiometric fluorescence probe was created through the physical combination of CDs and Eu-MOFs. Using the lanthanide ion Eu3+ as the central metal ion and the ligands NH2-BDC and H3TATAB, Eu-MOFs was synthesized in a single step hydrothermal process. The fluorescence intensity of Eu-MOFs (I448) increased whereas that of CDs (I568) decreased in the presence of AMX, as shown in Scheme 1 . The sensing signal was the ratio of fluorescence intensity at two distinct wavelengths. Similar to this, the I448/I568 difference was also seen in the fluorescence color change under a 365 nm UV lamp, enabling AMX to be visually determined using smartphone color software. The utilization of 3D hydrogel as sensing platform increased the sensitivity and selectivity of AMX evaluating. The proposed ratiometric sensor was successfully applied to determine the AMX concentration in various milk and water samples in liquid and hydrogel states.

All reagents in this experiment were analytical grade and used directly without further purification. Europium (III) chloride hexahydrate (EuCl3·6H2O), 4,4′,4″-s-triazine-1,3,5-triyltri-p-aminobenzoic acid (H3TATAB), 2-Aminoterephthalic acid (NH2-BDC), Dimethylformamide (DMF), O-Phenylenediamine (OPD), Carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC), Amoxicillin, Cephalexin, Ceftazidime, Clindamycin, Penicillin, Citric acid, Potassium chloride, Glucose, Calcium carbonate, Sodium chloride, Magnesium chloride, Copper chloride was obtained from (Sigma Aldrich). For all the experiments, distilled water with deionization was utilized.

The fluorescence spectra and the UV–Vis absorption spectra were measured at room temperature on a spectrophotometer (Varian Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer and a SPECTROD 250-analytikjena spectrophotometer (Germany) respectively. The morphology of product was characterized by scanning electron microscope (SEM) on a MIRA3 TESCAN HV: 20.0 KV from Czech Republic and the Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum was recorded on a Vector-22 BRUKER spectrophotometer (Switzerland).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of yellow emitting carbon dots (CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: CDs were synthesized using a previously reported procedure [39]. first, 500 mg of O-phenylenediamine (O-PD) were dissolved in 50 mL of water. Next, the solution was enclosed in a Teflon-lined autoclave and subjected to heating at 180 °C for a duration of 12 h. Following natural cooling, in the result suspension was centrifuged (4,000 r min−1, 15 min). The precipitate was separated and the obtained supernatant was utilized as fluorophore agent.

0.040 gs of EuCl3·6H2O and 0.145 gs of H3TATAB were dissolved in 10 ml of a 1:1 mixture of distilled water and DMF. The solution was stirred for 30 min. Then, 0.018 gs of NH2-BDC were added and mixed well for another 30 min. The resulting mixture was then transferred to a 20 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel vessel and heated at 120 °C for 72 h. After cooling to room temperature, the solid product was collected by centrifugation and thoroughly washed with deionized water and ethanol. Finally, the products were dried at 60 °C under vacuum overnight.

The fluorescent hydrogels were prepared according to a published method [40]. To begin, 0.75 g of Carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) was added to 15 mL of water and stirred for one hour until a uniform solution was obtained. Next, 100 μM of CDs@Eu-MOFs was introduced to the hydrogel and left for 2 h while being stirred, resulting in the formation of fluorescent hydrogels. Finally, various concentrations of AMX antibiotics were added to the hydrogel and stirred for 30 min. As a comparison, the CMC hydrogel without CDs@Eu-MOFs was prepared using the same method.

In this assay, after established that a CDs@Eu-MOFs aqueous solution at a concentration of 100μM would react with standard AMX solutions. The fluorescence intensity of the resulting mixture was then measured at 448 nm and 568 nm after excitation at 360 nm. The selectivity for AMX was evaluated with different ions (Na+, K +, Ca2+, Mg+2, Cu+2) and molecules (Ceftazidime, Penicillin, cephalexin, Clindamycin, citric acid, glucose).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of AMX in water and milk samples
ce:section.ce:para: Water and milk samples were collected from Sanandaj City in Iran. To eliminate suspended particles, centrifuged was used to separate the water and the supernatant was then filtered using a 0.22 μM microporous membrane to remove any impurities. The filtrate obtained was used for quantitative analysis. For the AMX analysis, a standard solution was prepared. Moreover, as for the milk samples, they were obtained from local markets and incorporated both low-fat and high-fat milk. Initially, 3 mL of each milk sample was mixed with 6 mL of distilled water in a 15 mL centrifuge tube. Then, a mixture of 10% (v/v) trichloroacetic acid and chloroform was added, and the resulting mixture was exposed to ultrasonic treatment for 15 min to eliminate the protein precipitate. The separation solution was then centrifuged at 7000 rpm for 15 min. The supernatant obtained was filtered using a 0.22 μM filter membrane to remove any lipids present [41]. To analyze the milk samples for AMX, different concentrations of AMX were added to the treated milk samples and directly used for FL analysis.

The morphology and structure of the synthesized CDs and Eu-MOFs were initially investigated using TEM and SEM techniques as illustrated in (Fig. 1 A,1B). The quantum yield (QY) of the carbon dots (CDs) calculated based on the relative method [42] using quinine sulfate as the reference. The obtained value of QY for the proposed CDs was 47%, details reported in SI and Table S1. The structure of CDs was specifically analyzed via TEM, revealing that the homogeneous nanoparticles have an average size ranging from 8–10 nm (average diameter of 8.9 nm). The fluorescence emission spectra of CDs, when excited at different wavelengths between 300 and 360 nm, showed a maximum emission intensity at 568 nm as shown in (Fig. 1C). The 2D excitation-emission counter mappings of the CDs that are depicted in the (Fig. 1D). On the other hand, SEM analysis of Eu-MOFs indicated a cubic structure. The pore size distribution diagram illustrated an average pore radius of approximately 80 nm for Eu-MOFs. Furthermore, the pore diameter width of Eu-MOFs was larger than the size of CDs, referring to successful encapsulation of CDs within the cavities of Eu-MOFs. The fluorescence emission spectra of Eu-MOFs, under varying excitation wavelengths from 300 to 360 nm, exhibited a maximum intensity at 448 nm showed in (Fig. 1E). Additionally, the 2D excitation-emission counter mappings of the Eu-MOFs are shown in the (Fig. 1F). The Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained and used for the analysis of ligands H3TATAB, NH2-BDC, and Eu-MOFs (mixed ligands) to verify the successful formation of the Eu-MOFs with dual ligands and to determine the presence functional groups on the surface of the synthesized Eu-MOFs (Fig. S1A). The ligand H3TATAB displayed peaks at 1602 and 1412 cm−1, corresponding to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of carbonyl groups. Similarly, in NH2BDC, peaks at 1622 cm−1 and 1421 cm−1 were recognized as C = O stretching vibrations of the carboxyl group (-COOH). Additionally, the infrared peaks observed at 3453 cm−1 and 3417 cm−1 in the spectra of Eu-MOFs, are related to the presence of NH2 groups. Moreover, the FT-IR analysis was employed to identify the functional groups present on the surface of CDs@Eu-MOFs in CMC hydrogel as depicted in (Fig. S1B). Analysis of the FT-IR spectra in hydrogel state revealed that the synthesized CDs@Eu-MOFs exhibited absorption bands similar to those of Eu-MOFs and CDs, suggesting the successful doping of CDs and Eu-MOFs in hydrogel. Moreover, X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was conducted to evaluate the crystallinity of Eu-MOFs. The presence of intense diffraction peaks in (Fig. 1SC) indicates the high degree of crystallinity of the material. Major crystalline peaks were observed at 2θ values of 5.80°, 8.82°, 11.72°, 11.83°, 17.50°, 26.80°, and 28.27° [43].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties, photostability and dispersity of CDs@Eu-MOFs
ce:section.ce:para: The optical characteristics of AMX, CDs, Eu-MOFs, and CDs@Eu-MOFs were analyzed through fluorescence and UV–vis absorption spectra in liquid and hydrogel states. The UV–visible absorption spectra exhibit a significant absorption peak at 260 nm, attended by a shoulder around 300 nm for carbon dots (CDs) as demonstrated in (Fig. S2A). The absorption peak at 260 nm signifies the π-π* transition of C = C bonds, whereas the peak at 300 nm could be related to the n-π* transition of the located amine groups on the surface of the CDs [44,45]. The fluorescence spectra of CDs@Eu-MOFs were recorded with excitation wavelengths ranging from 300 to 400 nm in liquid form and 310 to 370 nm in hydrogel form, as depicted in (Fig. 2 A, B). The emission fluorescence intensity of Eu-MOFs at 448 nm increased with excitation wavelength increased from 300 to 340 nm, but decreased as it increased from 340 to 400 nm. An excitation wavelength of 340 nm was determined as the most effective in generating the maximum emission intensity of Eu-MOFs at 448 nm. Additionally, (Fig. 2C) present the graphical representation of the 2D excitation-emission counter mappings for the CDs@Eu-MOFs. Conversely, the fluorescence intensity of CDs increased with the excitation wavelength rising from 310 to 370 nm, while 360 nm was selected for further investigations between CDs and MOFs. The fluorescence spectra of CDs@Eu-MOFs, CDs, and the Eu-MOF in both states exhibited emission peaks at 448 nm and 568 nm under 360 nm excitation. Moreover, the fluorescence spectra of CDs, Eu-MOFs and CDs@Eu-MOFs sensor for both states, shown in (Fig. 2D, 2E), indicate that both Eu-MOFs and CDs can be excited at 360 nm. It should be to note that the fluorescence intensity and peak position remained constant before and after the mixing process, suggesting that Eu-MOFs and CDs maintain their distinct optical properties without have an effect on each other.

The hydrogel's characterization in different forms such as CMC, CDs@Eu-MOFs-CMC, and AMX-CDs@Eu-MOFs-CMC is established using photographs. These photographs are taken under visible and UV light irradiation. Under visible light, the hydrogel color was white and changed to yellow when it modified with AMX and CDs@Eu-MOFs. In contrast, under UV light, the color of the hydrogel changes to gray, while the CDs@Eu-MOFs-CMC and AMX-CDs@Eu-MOFs-CMC hydrogel exhibits a green coloration as shown in (Fig. 2F).

The fluorescence quenching mechanism of CDs materials is principally ascribed to four factors: static quenching, fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), inner filter effect (IFE), and photoinduced electron transfer (PET) [45]. To investigate the inner filter effect (IFE) mechanism, which originates from the overlap of the absorption spectrum of the analyte and the excitation/emission spectrum of the fluorophore [46], we analyzed UV/vis absorption spectrum analysis of AMX and excitation of the CDs as indicates in (Fig. S3A). AMX exhibits a broad characteristic absorption ranging from 220 nm to 280 nm, partially overlapping with the excitation spectrum of CDs. The absorption peak of AMX at 270 nm effectively hinders the excitation energy absorption of CDs, resulting in significant quenching of the CDs fluorescence. On the other hand, the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CDs coincides with the emission peak of the Eu-MOFs, indicating the potential for energy transfer from CDs to Eu-MOFs. This transfer causes a decrease in the fluorescence intensity of CDs as the donor molecules and an increase in the fluorescence intensity of Eu-MOFs as the acceptor probe, leading to notable quenching of the CDs fluorescence through the Förster resonance energy transfer mechanism (FRET) [47]. Moreover, AMX can collaborate with Eu3+ and serve as an "antenna" to transfer the absorbed energy to Eu3+. Additionally, AMX acts as a perfect ligand for Eu3+, forming an AMX–Eu3+ complex that enhances the fluorescence of Eu3+. Consequently, the integration of AMX and Eu3+ may result in the fluorescence enhancement of the emission peak at 448 nm, as proved by the simultaneous increase in the spectrum of Eu-MOFs and decrease in the spectrum of CDs in the presence of AMX.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The ratiometric fluorescence sensing of AMX in both liquid and hydrogel states
ce:section.ce:para: The performance of the proposed sensor was evaluated by testing its reaction to AMX under optimal experimental conditions. The fluorescence intensity of Eu-MOFs at 448 nm exhibited a graduate increase with the increase in AMX concentration. Conversely, the fluorescence intensity of CDs at 568 nm indicated a gradual decrease. These observations are depicted in (Fig. 3 A and 3B) for both states. The ratio I448/I568 and the concentration of AMX showed a linear correlation within the range of (10–106.7μM) in a liquid state, as illustrated in (Fig. 3C). The linear regression equation for this correlation can be expressed as I448/I568 = 0.0056x + 0.3116 [AMX], with a linear correlation coefficient of 0.993. The detection limit was determined to be 1.17 μM. Furthermore, the prepared CDs@Eu-MOFs were also utilized for AMX detection in hydrogel state as demonstrated in (Fig. 3D), exhibiting a linear range of (0.3–3.07μM). The linear regression equation for this correlation can be expressed as I448/I568 = 0.1802x + 0.5268 [AMX], with a linear correlation coefficient of 0.9909. The detection limit in this case was found to be 0.08 μM (S/N = 3). These results indicate the sensitivity of the sensing assay improved more than 32 folds for hydrogel compared to conventional solution state.

However, CDs in the hydrogel state exhibited enhanced stability compared with liquid state. When Eu3+ ions were mixed with CDs and exposure increased, coordination improved and enhanced ability to detect AMX. Additionally, the stability of the hydrogel was considered through its keeping even after 100 h in typical laboratory conditions, as depicted in (Fig. 3E, Fig. S3B). Moreover, the collected data from the done experiment indicates a consistent fluorescence intensity ratio in the liquid state, ranging from 0 to 40 min as shown in (Fig. 3F, Fig S3C). This highlights that the stability of the hydrogel state surpasses that of the liquid state. Moreover, the sensor exhibited high sensitivity and selectivity, effectively minimizing various interferences. When compared to other published analytical techniques for AMX detection, as presented in (Table 1A ), CDs@Eu-MOFs demonstrated a low detection limit and a wide range of detection capabilities.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The visualization and prompt identification of AMX
ce:section.ce:para: The designed sensor not only shows a high level of sensitivity and strong anti-interference capabilities, but also allows for the visual identification of AMX. The CDs@Eu-MOFs solution exhibited a white to yellow color in visible light and green fluorescence under a 365 nm UV lamp. Upon the addition of various concentrations of AMX, the solutions color shows a noticeable transformation from white-yellow to green. These outcomes suggest that the CDs@Eu-MOFs solution its very convenient as ratiometric fluorescent probes for the visual identification of AMX. Furthermore, visual detection methods using the naked eye do not support on-site quantitative analysis of AMX. To address this problem, we employed a smartphone and a 365 nm UV lamp as a portable sensing platform. As depicted in (Fig. 4 A,4B), the smartphone equipped with an RGB Color Detector (color recognizer application) can translate the fluorescence colors into color data (RGB value). The red channel (R), green channel (G), and the Blue Channel (B) were used to compute the RGB ratio. A significant linear relationship was noted between G/mean(R + G + B) and AMX concentration up to 105 μM, with the corresponding linear equation being G/mean(R + G + B) = 0.0114[AMX]+0.973 (R2= 0.9816) and minimum detectable concentration was 0.63 μM. This suggests that the smartphone-assisted sensing platform views as great promise for the real-time quantitative detection of AMX.

The selectivity of the method was estimated by evaluating the fluorescence response of CDs@Eu-MOFs under optimized conditions, as shown in (Fig. 4C and 4D). To test the selectivity, various interfering substances were chosen, including common metal ions (Na+, K +, Ca2+, Mg+2 and Cu+2), representative saccharide, amino acids, and antibiotics (Ceftazidime, Clindamycin, Penicillin, Cephalexin, glucose, and citric acid). These materials were tested at concentrations of 100 μM and 10 μM for the liquid and hydrogel states, respectively. The concentration of AMX was held at 10 μM and 1.0 μM for both methods. Among the tested interfering substances, only AMX revealed a significant fluorescence response to the ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on CDs@Eu-MOFs, while the other components showed negligible fluorescence response. Additionally, there were no significant changes in the luminescence of the CDs@Eu-MOFs system, except in presence of AMX. These findings indicate that the CDs@Eu-MOFs system have exceptional selectivity for the detection of AMX. Furthermore, the hydrogel state exhibits higher selectivity compared to the liquid state. So, the application of hydrogel as sensing platform not only increased the sensitivity but also the selectivity is also improved.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of amoxicillin in samples of milk and water
ce:section.ce:para: The evaluation of AMX in real samples was conducted by spiking milk and water with varying concentrations of the analyte AMX in both liquid and hydrogel states. The presented data in Table 1B revealed that the concentrations of AMX in the spiked water and milk samples were measured at 10, 56.5, and 106.7 μM (n = 3) and 0.32, 1.57, 3.07 μM (n = 3), with recovery rates ranging from 94 to 104% and 96 to 103% for liquid and hydrogel states respectively. These findings emphasize the reliability, accuracy, and consistency of the proposed sensor when applied to real samples. The results of the test indicate a promising potential for the application of the developed ratiometric fluorescent probe in the detection of AMX in water and milk samples.

In this investigation a new approach was developed to detect AMX in both liquid and hydrogel states by combining fluorescence and colorimetry methods. A unique ratiometric fluorescence sensing method was designed to accurately measure AMX levels in local milk samples and water using CDs@Eu-MOFs dual system emitted light at 448 nm and 568 nm simultaneously within the carboxymethyl cellos (CMC) when excited at 360 nm. This dual-emission property enabled the generation of a ratiometric fluorescent signal for detection of AMX. The method exhibited high sensitivity towards AMX, with a wide linear range of 10–106.7 µM and a detection limit of 1.17 µM in liquid samples. Similarly, in hydrogel samples, the method demonstrated excellent sensitivity, with a broad linear range of 0.3–3.07 μM and a detection limit of 0.08 µM. It should be noted, the hydrogel assay exhibited a 32-fold increase in sensitivity compared to traditional immunoassays performed in liquid samples. Moreover, not only the selectivity improved but also the stability of the signal response in the hydrogel state was significantly higher than in the liquid state. The biosensor also offered advantages such as affordability, simplicity and minimal interference, making it a suitable tool for the quantitative measurement of residual antibiotics in various real samples.

Kurdistan Fakhraldin Azeez: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation. Abdollah Salimi: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization. Hamed Mohtasham: Formal analysis, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research supported by the Research Office of University of Kurdistan (grant number 01–9–19724–135).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.snr.2024.100262.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A robust cluster-based Eu-MOF as multi-functional fluorescence sensor for detection of antibiotics and pesticides in water
Sensors Actuators B Chem
